questions.text: The electric flux through the surface d of a sphere of radius "p" due to a point charge a placed at its center depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to Gauss’s Law, flux is independent of radius.

options.text: The surface area of the sphere
options.explanation: Again, Gauss’s Law states flux only depends on enclosed charge.

options.text: The quantity of charge outside of the sphere
options.explanation: External charges don’t affect net flux inside a closed surface.

options.text: The quantity of charge inside of the sphere
options.explanation: Flux = Q_enclosed / ϵ₀ — depends only on the charge inside the sphere.

questions.explanation: Flux = Q_enclosed / ϵ₀ — depends only on the charge inside the sphere.

questions.text: An electric field lines provide the Information about:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Field lines show direction and strength of force on a test charge.
Field lines are tangent to the electric field vector at any point.
Density and behavior of field lines may vary with medium.
All above statements are true — so “All of these” is the most complete

questions.explanation: All above statements are true — so “All of these” is the most complete.

questions.text: The electric field is uniform:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The field radiates outward and is not uniform.
Field is radial and non-uniform.

options.text: Between two parallel oppositely charged plates
options.explanation: Field lines are parallel and evenly spaced — indicating uniformity.
Field near an edge or a single plate is not perfectly uniform.

questions.explanation: Field lines are parallel and evenly spaced — indicating uniformity.

questions.text: Capacitor is a device used for:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Capacitors store energy/charge, not voltage directly.
Current is a flow, not stored; capacitors resist changes in it.
Capacitors store electric charge on their plates.
Capacitors don’t create charge — they store what is supplied.

questions.explanation: Capacitors store electric charge on their plates.

questions.text: Force on a charged particle due to an electrostatic field is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Gravity is a separate fundamental force; unrelated to charge.
This is the force described by Coulomb’s Law acting between charges.
Acts on moving charges due to magnetic fields, not static ones.
Acts inside the nucleus; irrelevant to electrostatics.

questions.explanation: This is the force described by Coulomb’s Law acting between charges.

questions.text: Which of the following is the property of electrostatic field lines?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Electrostatic field lines always start on positive charge and end on negative charges
options.explanation: This is a fundamental rule — field lines show the path a positive test charge would take. They begin at positive charges and terminate at negative charges.

options.text: The number of lines that originate from, or terminate on a charge is proportional to the magnitude of the charge
options.explanation: A stronger charge will have more field lines. For example, a +2q charge will emit twice as many lines as a +q charge.

options.text: The direction of the field at a point is along the tangent to the line of force
options.explanation: The direction of the electric field vector at any point is tangent to the field line at that point. This shows how a test charge would move.
All of the above are correct, so the answer is (d).

questions.explanation: All of the above are correct, so the answer is (d).

questions.text: An electron of charge e coulomb passes through a potential difference of V volts. Its energy in 'Joules' will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This looks like a typo or formatting issue; not a standard physical expression.
The work done or energy gained by a charge q when it moves through a potential difference V is:For an electron (charge = e), energy = e × V joules.
This suggests dividing charge by potential, which gives units of capacitance (Farads), not energy.
V is the potential, not energy. Energy must be charge × potential.

questions.explanation: The work done or energy gained by a charge q when it moves through a potential difference V is:For an electron (charge = e), energy = e × V joules.

options.explanation: Field inside is zero.

questions.explanation: Inside a conducting shell, electric field = 0 due to electrostatic shielding.
